key_points: The Earth rotates on its tilted axis, which together with revolution causes seasonal variations
The Moon's revolution around the Earth and its position relative to the Sun cause the different phases of the Moon
Eclipses occur when the Earth, Moon and Sun align in a straight line
A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon comes between the Earth and the Sun
A lunar eclipse occurs when the Earth comes between the Sun and the Moon
examples: Solar eclipse occurs during the new moon phase
Lunar eclipse occurs during the full moon phase
Phases of the Moon repeat in a regular cycle
The tilt of the Earth's axis affects sunlight distribution across the planet
formulas: Rotation → Earth spinning on its axis once every 24 hours
Revolution → Earth moving around the Sun once every 365 days
Moon cycle → Approximately 29.5 days for complete phase cycle
exam_tips: Clearly differentiate between solar and lunar eclipses
Understand the causes of Moon phases using diagrams
Remember the role of Earth's tilt in seasonal changes
Practice labelled diagrams showing Earth, Moon and Sun positions
